speaker: Unknown
en: Whoever's in the lead, it feels very obvious to me that all of the work that you do is going to be in one of those surfaces where, for example, when I'm writing a document, Codex has a browser in the app.
zh: 无论谁处于领先地位，对我来说都非常明显，你做的所有工作都将在其中一个界面中进行，例如，当我写文档时，Codex 在应用程序里有一个浏览器。

speaker: Unknown
en: It has an in-app browser.
zh: 它有一个应用内浏览器。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when I'm writing a document, I just go into one of my Codex threads, which I have one thread for every project, and I just open the in-app browser, I go to the document, I usually do it in Proof, which is this online markdown editor I built.
zh: 当我写文档时，我只是进入我的 Codex 线程之一，我为每个项目都有一个线程，我只需打开应用内浏览器，我进入文档，我通常在 Proof 中做，这是我构建的在线 markdown 编辑器。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I just have Codex running and watching me in Proof and Codex can see what I'm doing.
zh: 然后我就让 Codex 运行并在 Proof 中监视我，Codex 能看到我在做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: I can see what Codex is doing.
zh: 我能看到 Codex 在做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's all in one place, which is an extension of the same thing that made Claude Code work really well originally.
zh: 这一切都在一个地方，这是让 Claude Code 最初运作得很好的同一事物的延伸。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I basically feel like I have this parallel work buddy that not only can it respond and write in the document, but then it can go do research, it can use my computer to basically do anything that I can do on my computer and that's incredibly powerful.
zh: 我基本上觉得我有这个并行的工作伙伴，它不仅可以在文档中响应和写作，而且它可以去进行研究，它可以使用我的计算机基本上做我在计算机上能做的任何事情，这非常强大。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I do this with everything.
zh: 我对所有事情都这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've been at inbox zero for 10 days straight now, which if you know me, is crazy.
zh: 我已经连续 10 天保持收件箱清零状态了，如果你了解我，这太疯狂了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm never like this, and that's because I literally just have Codex gather all my emails with Quora, which is our email agent.
zh: 我从来没有像这样过，因为我真的只是让 Codex 与 Quora 收集我所有的电子邮件，Quora 是我们的电子邮件智能体。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then it renders a little page, and I think I showed you this at the Anthropic event.
zh: 然后它渲染一个小页面，我想我在 Anthropic 活动上给你看过这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: It renders a little page and I just monologue into it and just talk out each email.
zh: 它渲染一个小页面，我只是对着它独白，把每封邮件说出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm like, "Okay, go research this.
zh: 我就像，“好的，去研究这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, here's a question from our lawyers.
zh: 哦，这是我们律师的一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Can you go collect all of the documents from the last four years and then put them into a report and send them?"
zh: 你能去收集过去四年的所有文档，然后把它们放进一个报告并发送出去吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it just does it.
zh: 然后它就做到了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so all the stuff that I would procrastinate on, I don't really procrastinate on anymore.
zh: 所以我所有会拖延的事情，我真的不再拖延了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I feel like there's this, for a long time, I thought too that the optimal experience of AI was going to be take AI and put it in a browser.
zh: 所以我觉得有这个，很长一段时间，我也认为 AI 的最佳体验是将 AI 放入浏览器中。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the reverse is actually starting to happen and be really, really valuable in a way that I did not expect, which is take the AI agent that you use all the time on your computer and put a browser in it so it can see everything you're doing.
zh: 我认为相反的情况实际上开始发生，并且真的、真的有价值，以一种我没有预料到的方式，那就是把你一直使用的 AI 智能体放在你的计算机上，并在其中放入一个浏览器，这样它就能看到你在做的一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that is just like a magical combination that I think is very uncommon now.
zh: 这是一个神奇的组合，我认为现在非常少见。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't even do this in Claude Code because they don't let you browse external websites inside of Claude Code.
zh: 你甚至不能在 Claude Code 中做到这一点，因为他们不允许你在 Claude Code 内部浏览外部网站。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's very uncommon now, but I think it will be super common in a year.
zh: 所以这现在很少见，但我认为一年后它会变得非常普遍。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is more profound than it may even sound.
zh: 这比听起来可能更深刻。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I'm hearing is instead of AI being baked into SaaS tools, what you're predicting here is the SaaS tools will run within Codex or Claude Code?
zh: 我听到的是，AI 不是被嵌入到 SaaS 工具中，你在这里预测的是 SaaS 工具将在 Codex 或 Claude Code 中运行？

speaker: Dan Shipper
en: That is one-
zh: 这是一个——

speaker: Unknown
en: ... code.

speaker: Unknown
en: That is one really important second order effect of this is ...
zh: 这是一个非常重要的二阶效应，它是……

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, so yeah, I'm using Proof or really any website, maybe PostHog or whatever, and I'm doing it inside of my agent and the agent has access to the website.
zh: 好的，是的，我正在使用 Proof 或实际上是任何网站，也许是 PostHog 或其他的，我在我的智能体内部做这件事，智能体可以访问该网站。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, it has access to everything that I have access to, and it has access to my whole computer.
zh: 所以，它可以访问我有权访问的一切，它可以访问我的整个计算机。

speaker: Unknown
en: When I run the agent on that website, I'm using my tokens.
zh: 当我在该网站上运行智能体时，我使用的是我的 token。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm not using the vendor's tokens, I'm not using the app's tokens.
zh: 我没有使用供应商的 token，我没有使用应用程序的 token。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, it puts SaaS back in this place where yeah, you want to make it friendly for an agent and everyone's got a CLI now, you want to make the HTML really usable.
zh: 所以，它把 SaaS 带回到了一个地方，是的，你想让它对智能体友好，现在每个人都有一个 CLI，你想让 HTML 真正可用。

speaker: Unknown
en: You want to make sure that anything that happens in the CLI shows up for the user immediately, all that kind of stuff.
zh: 你想确保 CLI 中发生的任何事情都立即为用户显示，所有这类事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are a lot of issues to deal with.
zh: 有很多问题需要处理。

speaker: Unknown
en: But once you do that, you actually don't really need to think about having an AI surface that's primarily going to be the thing that users use in the sense that you don't need to build an agent necessarily into your product.
zh: 但是一旦你做到了，你实际上真的不需要考虑拥有一个 AI 界面，而那个界面主要将是用户使用的东西，因为你不需要 necessarily 将智能体构建到你的产品中。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think you can, and there's another really interesting bifurcation of this that we should talk about, which is that having two agents is better than one.
zh: 我认为你可以，还有另一个非常有趣的分支我们应该谈谈，那就是拥有两个智能体比一个更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think for now there's this really cool thing where with Proof, for example, anyone who uses it, I don't pay for tokens because they bring their AI to Proof.
zh: 但我认为目前有这个很酷的事情，例如 Proof，任何使用它的人，我不支付 token 费用，因为他们把他们的 AI 带到了 Proof。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, it changes what you build as a SaaS company and you build it now for both humans and agents to use at the same time, and it changes your margins back to, well, I don't really have to pay for tokens anymore because the user's going to bring AI.
zh: 所以，这改变你作为 SaaS 公司构建的东西，你现在构建它是为了人类和智能体同时使用，它把你的利润率改回，嗯，我真的不需要支付 token 费用了，因为用户会带来 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, I think this is a huge deal.
zh: 所以，我认为这是一件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, what you're describing here is more and more work that we do, more and more professional work, is it just going to happen within Codex or Claude Code?
zh: 那么，你在这里描述的是越来越多的我们所做的工作，越来越多的专业工作，它只是会在 Codex 或 Claude Code 中发生吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Where does Cursor fit into this?
zh: Cursor 适合放在哪里？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there potential there?
zh: 有潜力吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a good question.
zh: 这是一个好问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that Cursor sees a lot of the same stuff, and in some ways they have some of the same stuff but it's better.
zh: 我认为 Cursor 看到了很多相同的东西，在某些方面他们有一些相同的东西，但更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that Cursor's cloud implementation is better than either OpenAIs or Anthropics and is more advanced, and I think that Cursor has at least so far more distinctly chosen a lane.
zh: 我认为 Cursor 的云实现比 OpenAI 或 Anthropic 的都要好，而且更先进，我认为 Cursor 至少到目前为止更明确地选择了一个车道。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're more distinctly choosing to be for programmers, and that may limit how far they get in here.
zh: 他们更明确地选择是为程序员服务的，这可能会限制他们在这里能走多远。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the definition programmer is expanding enough that they'll have a big market, but I don't know that they're going to jump into like, "Okay, use this to make a slide deck," or whatever.
zh: 我认为程序员的定义正在扩展，他们会有一个大市场，但我不确定他们会不会跳进像，“好的，用这个来做幻灯片演示文稿”之类的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it is really clear that every model company is starting to realize how important it is to have a harness to get the most out of the model.
zh: 但非常清楚的是，每个模型公司都开始意识到拥有一个挽具以充分利用模型是多么重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, where all the platforms are moving is to a world where you're not just doing prompt and response.
zh: 所以，所有平台正在迈向的方向是这样一个世界，在这个世界里，你不仅仅是在做提示和响应。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you call the model on the OpenAI platform, the Anthropic platform, they're literally running the model on a computer that is in the cloud that they run and then giving you the result out of it.
zh: 当你在 OpenAI 平台、Anthropic 平台上调用模型时，它们实际上是在一台由他们运行在云端的计算机上运行该模型，然后将其结果给你。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they know that in order to get the best results of the model they need to offer that.
zh: 而且他们知道，为了获得模型的最佳结果，他们需要提供那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, you see Anthropic's got cloud managed agents.
zh: 所以，你看 Anthropic 拥有云端托管智能体。

speaker: Unknown
en: OpenAI does not have a response yet, but I assume that that's going to happen.
zh: OpenAI 还没有回应，但我假设那将会发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now Cursor was just essentially acquired by SpaceX.
zh: 现在 Cursor 刚刚基本上被 SpaceX 收购了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not like a full acquisition, but it's close.
zh: 这不像是一个完整的收购，但很接近了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, I think people are starting to realize I can't just do the model part of it, I have to have this harness above it.
zh: 所以，我认为人们开始意识到我不能只做模型这部分，我必须得有它上面的这个挽具。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the ultimate form of that harness is like I can do any kind of knowledge work.
zh: 我认为那个挽具的终极形式就像是我能做任何种类的知识工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Cursor itself feels like one of the things that it's going to be a hard decision for them whether to stay just for coders or not.
zh: Cursor 本身感觉像是其中一件事，对它们来说是否只面向程序员将是一个艰难的决定。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, if people building products that aren't OpenAI or Anthropic, if this proves to be true, the prediction here is they're going to be using your product over time inside of one of these agents.
zh: 所以，如果那些构建产品的公司不是 OpenAI 或 Anthropic，如果这被证明是真的，这里的预测是它们随着时间的推移会在这些智能体中的一个里面使用你的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there something you would do if you were one of those companies to prepare for that future?
zh: 如果你是这些公司中的一家，你会做些什么来为那个未来做准备吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I would just prepare for that.
zh: 我只会为此做准备。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, every more classic piece of productivity software, whether it's Slack, or Word docs, or PowerPoints or whatever, it's really mostly meant for a human to use.
zh: 所以举个例子，每一个更经典的生产力软件，无论是 Slack，还是 Word 文档，或 PowerPoint 还是别的什么，它真的主要是 meant 给人类使用的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now people are doing CLI so it's meant for an agent to use independently of a human.
zh: 现在人们正在做 CLI，所以它是 meant 给智能体独立于人类使用的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we're moving into this new paradigm, I think, where the human and the agent are on the same piece of work together, and they're both doing things, and I need to have visibility into what the agent is doing, the agent has to have visibility into what I'm doing.
zh: 我认为我们正在进入这个新范式，在这个范式里人类和智能体在同一份工作上一起，它们都在做事情，我需要能看见智能体在做什么，智能体必须能看见我在做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have to go back and forth in this seamless way.
zh: 我们必须以这种无缝的方式来回往复。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the kind of software that you make for that is going to be very different.
zh: 你为那个制作的软件种类将会非常不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, there's a lot of stuff that Proof doesn't have.
zh: 所以举个例子，有很多东西是 Proof 没有的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't have to have a lot of the Word document formatting, or page breaks, or making tables or whatever, because the agent just does it.
zh: 我不必有很多 Word 文档格式，或分页符，或制作表格或别的什么，因为智能体只是做它。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't need to worry about that, it can do all the formatting for me.
zh: 我不需要担心那个，它可以为我做所有的格式化。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, you can make the products a lot simpler and faster to start than the legacy products are.
zh: 所以，你可以让产品比传统产品简单得多、启动得快得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then, there's all these other affordances that you need to start to have because the way agents interact with software is very different.
zh: 然后，还有很多其他的“affordances”（供给/ affordances）你需要开始有，因为智能体与软件交互的方式非常不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, agents can do a lot at once.
zh: 所以举个例子，智能体一次能做很多事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: They can just do a billion different things to your document, or your slide deck, or your code base or whatever, and how you display that to the user is going to be very different than the way you might display a human being concurrent in your document and doing stuff.
zh: 它们可以对你的文档，或你的幻灯片组，或你的代码库或别的什么做十亿件不同的事情，而且你如何向用户展示那个将非常不同于你可能展示一个人同时出现在你的文档里并在做事情的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need approval, you need a inbox that summarizes, "Here's all the stuff that's going to happen," or has happened.
zh: 你需要批准，你需要一个收件箱来总结，“这是所有要发生的事情”，或已经发生的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need logs and the ability to roll it back real quick.
zh: 你需要日志和快速回滚的能力。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, there's all those kinds of considerations that change the actual product, and then the underlying UX of it or the underlying infrastructure you need is different too, because agents can make a billion requests in three seconds.
zh: 所以，有所有这些种类的考虑改变了实际的产品，然后它的底层 UX 或你需要的底层基础设施也不同，因为智能体可以在三秒钟内发出十亿次请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, how are you going to deal with that?
zh: 所以，你要如何处理那个？

speaker: Unknown
en: This is exactly why GitHub is having problems right now because the number of people using GitHub is skyrocketing exponentially and it's really just people's agencies in GitHub.
zh: 这正是 GitHub 现在有问题的原因，因为使用 GitHub 的人数正在呈指数级飙升，而这实际上只是人们的智能体在 GitHub 上。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, I think it's this whole new world that is just starting, you're just starting to see a little peek of it, but there's so many cool things about it.
zh: 所以，我认为这是一个刚刚开始的全新世界，你才刚刚开始看到它的一瞥，但它有很多很酷的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, in Proof and some of our other products too, when someone has a problem they don't email support.
zh: 所以举个例子，在 Proof 和我们的其他一些产品里，当某人遇到问题时他们不会给支持发邮件。

speaker: Unknown
en: Their agent sends a bug report, and an agent bug report is way better than a human bug report.
zh: 他们的智能体发送一个错误报告，而且一个智能体错误报告比人类错误报告好得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: It has like, "Here's exactly what I did, here's the exact repro steps, here's like ..."
zh: 它有像，“这是我确切做的，这是确切的复现步骤，这里有像……”

speaker: Unknown
en: Proof is open source so, "Here's what I think is going on in the code base."
zh: Proof 是开源的所以，“这是我认为代码库里正在发生的事情。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then we just get that, it becomes a GitHub issue, and then we can just send off an agent to fix it.
zh: 然后我们就收到那个，它变成了一个 GitHub issue，然后我们可以直接发送一个智能体去修复它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you can't do that with everything but it's so much better, and you can see the glimmers of this very fast closed loop between I ran into something, a paper cut, a little feature I want, a little bug, and my agent just goes off and talks to the company agent and then the company agent just goes and fixes it.
zh: 你不能对所有事情都做那个，但它好得多，而且你可以看到这个非常快的闭环的微光，在我遇到了某事，一个“paper cut”（小麻烦），我想要的一个小功能，一个小 bug，和我的智能体只是离开去和公司的智能体交谈然后公司的智能体只是去修复它之间。

speaker: Unknown
en: That I think is incredibly cool.
zh: 我认为那是非常酷的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, as a part of this that a lot of people are moving to CLI and trying to work from the terminal, is part of this prediction that people shift away from that and back to actual UX with agents-
zh: 所以，作为这的一部分，很多人正在转向 CLI 并尝试从终端工作，这个预测的一部分是人们远离那个并回到实际的 UX 和智能体一起——

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, yeah.
zh: 哦，是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... running alongside them?
zh: ...在旁边运行吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: CLIs are over.
zh: CLIs 结束了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We speed ran the CLI era.
zh: 我们速通了 CLI 时代。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was nice while it lasted, but I think it's pretty clear ...
zh: 它在持续的时候很好，但我认为这很清楚……

speaker: Unknown
en: Sorry.
zh: 抱歉。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not that CLIs are going to completely go away.
zh: 不是说 CLIs 将会完全消失。

speaker: Unknown
en: Obviously they've been around for the last like 30 years, or 40 years or 50 years or whatever.
zh: 显然它们已经存在了大概 30 年，或 40 年或 50 年或别的什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: They will continue to be around.
zh: 它们将继续存在。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think there is this moment when Claude Code was so popular, or when Claude Code was really starting to gain in popularity that people were like, "The thing that's working is the fact that it's the CLI," and I don't think that's what it is.
zh: 我认为有这么一刻，当 Claude Code 是如此受欢迎，或当 Claude Code 真的开始获得普及，人们像，“正在起作用的事情是它是 CLI 这个事实”，而我不认为那是它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when you move into an actual UI for this, you start to realize we made GUIs for a reason, and it's just nicer to be in a GUI, and you can get all the same benefits inside of a GUI, especially for non-programmer work.
zh: 当你为此进入一个实际的 UI，你开始意识到我们制造 GUIs 是有原因的，而且在 GUI 里只是更好，而且你可以在一个 GUI 里获得所有同样的好处，特别是对于非程序员工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I would estimate that definitely the majority of the technical people inside of Every are not using CLIs anymore as their main work surface.
zh: 但我估计，Every 内部绝大多数技术人员肯定不再使用 CLI 作为他们的主要工作界面了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think a lot of programmers are still flipping into it every once in a while, but it's more or less they're using Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, that kind of thing.
zh: 我想很多程序员仍然会偶尔切换进去，但他们或多或少都在使用 Codex、Claude Code、Cursor 之类的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome, okay.
zh: 太棒了，好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I definitely wanted to make that part clear.
zh: 我确实想把那部分说清楚。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, coming back to the big picture of the prediction here, there's these two modes of work that you're anticipating.
zh: 那么，回到这里的预测大局，你预期有两种工作模式。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is this super agent within a company that you chat with through Slack most likely that can go off and do work and answer questions, and then there's on your computer running Codex or Claude Code.
zh: 一种是公司内部的这种超级智能体，你很可能通过 Slack 与它聊天，它可以去执行工作并回答问题，另一种是在你的电脑上运行 Codex 或 Claude Code。

speaker: Unknown
en: And within that, all the work that you normally do on your computers now are going to be living within Codex or Claude Code, or maybe some third party that emerges that we're not even aware of yet.
zh: 在其中，你现在通常在电脑上做的所有工作都将生活在 Codex 或 Claude Code 中，或者可能是我们尚未意识到的某个出现的第三方。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you're going to use apps inside of the internal browser of those tools.
zh: 你将要在这些工具的内部浏览器中使用应用程序。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow, okay.
zh: 哇，好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Listening to you talk about it, it may not feel as profound as it is, because this is a big change to how we work.
zh: 听你谈论它，感觉可能没有它实际上那么深远，因为这是我们工作方式的重大改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't currently have an AI that we talk to regularly in Slack, and we also don't work currently mostly in Codex or Claude Code.
zh: 我们目前还没有一个我们在 Slack 中定期交流的 AI，而且我们也目前主要不在 Codex 或 Claude Code 中工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, this is actually a pretty massive shift.
zh: 所以，这实际上是一个相当巨大的转变。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think so.
zh: 我认为是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there anything else along these lines before we get into our next prediction?
zh: 在我们进入下一个预测之前，在这方面还有其他事情吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, a few things.
zh: 嗯，有几件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm definitely not an agent maximalist, I really think we're going to have a lot of different agents that we use.
zh: 我绝对不是一个智能体最大化主义者，我真的认为我们将要使用许多不同的智能体。

speaker: Unknown
en: It seems pretty clear to me.
zh: 这对我来说似乎很清楚。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I really do think that two agents are better than one, so what's a good example?
zh: 而且我真的认为两个智能体胜过一个，那么有什么好例子呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: When I have Codex interact with another agent, it can give so much more context about me and what I want than I would be able to type, and it can go back and forth talking about things that would take a long time for me to express directly to an agent that you get this speed up effect when you assume that your users are using Codex, or Claude Code, or Cowork as their basic way they access your app.
zh: 当我让 Codex 与另一个智能体交互时，它可以给出关于我以及我想要的上下文，比我能够打字表达的要多得多，而且它可以来回谈论那些我需要很长时间才能直接向智能体表达的事情，当你假设你的用户正在使用 Codex、Claude Code 或 Cowork 作为他们访问你应用程序的基本方式时，你会获得这种加速效应。

speaker: Unknown
en: And a really simple example, we have this hosted OpenClaw product, which we had on waitlist.
zh: 一个非常简单的例子，我们有这个托管的 OpenClaw 产品，我们把它放在了等待名单上。

speaker: Unknown
en: We actually had to pause it because we started taking people off the waitlist, and OpenClaw is just a very a very hard agent harness to make work.
zh: 我们实际上不得不暂停它，因为我们开始让人们离开等待名单，而 OpenClaw 是一个非常难以工作的智能体装置。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's moving so incredibly fast, and if you're like a platform for it, it's like when things break you can't fix it.
zh: 它的移动速度非常难以置信，如果你是它的一个平台，就像当东西坏了你无法修复它一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very hard.
zh: 这非常困难。

speaker: Unknown
en: But one of the things that we learned in that process is let's say you're building an agent product or any new software experience, what you would assume, let's say to set up an agent, is you need to build a little web interface or a little Slack workflow that asks people about, "Okay, who are you and what are you going to use this for?
zh: 但我们在那个过程中学到的一件事是，假设你正在构建一个智能体产品或任何新的软件体验，你会假设，比如说设置一个智能体，是你需要构建一个小的 Web 界面或一个小的 Slack 工作流，询问人们关于，“好的，你是谁，你要用这个做什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And what's your ideal dream outcome?"
zh: 以及“你理想的梦想结果是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Or whatever the things you are that you would put on an onboarding checklist.
zh: 或者任何你可能放在入门清单上的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: If instead you just make a hard line of we are only going to service users who use Codex or Cowork, what happens is you just paste a prompt into Codex or Cowork, it goes and talks to the app, and the app can be either just a regular server or it can be its own agent, and Codex has so much information about you that it can just give it.
zh: 相反，如果你只是制定一条硬性规定，我们只为使用 Codex 或 Cowork 的用户服务，发生的事情是你只需将一个提示词粘贴到 Codex 或 Cowork 中，它去与应用程序交谈，该应用程序可以只是一个常规服务器，也可以是它自己的智能体，而 Codex 有关于你的大量信息，它可以直接给出它。

speaker: Unknown
en: "Here's all the stuff I've been working on with Dan, here's all the ways that he might want to use this app," and then bring it back to me, and it's this very custom experience.
zh: “这是我和 Dan 一直在做的所有事情，这是他可能想要使用这个应用程序的所有方式，”然后把它带回来给我，这是一种非常定制的体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: And also for a technical product like an agent, when something goes wrong I can just tell Codex, "Go fix it."
zh: 而且对于像智能体这样的技术产品，当出现问题时我可以直接告诉 Codex，“去修复它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And Codex will go talk to the app and figure out what's going on for me.
zh: Codex 将去与应用程序交谈并为我弄清楚发生了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, I think the whole paradigm starts to change when you assume that everyone's got an agent and those agents are talking to other agents in this really magical and important way.
zh: 所以，我认为当你假设每个人都有一个智能体，并且这些智能体以这种非常神奇和重要的方式与其他智能体交谈时，整个范式开始改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a couple more things I want to touch on before we get started, because there's so much to talk about.
zh: 在我们开始之前还有几件事我想触及，因为有太多事情要谈论了。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is you made this point about SaaS tools not using ... You can use tokens from the model companies basically when using a SaaS tool.
zh: 一是你提出了关于 SaaS 工具不使用……的观点。当使用 SaaS 工具时，你基本上可以使用模型公司提供的 token。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk a bit more about that, because that may change the business model for SaaS companies in the future.
zh: 多谈谈那一点，因为那可能会在未来改变 SaaS 公司的商业模式。

speaker: Unknown
en: That feels like a big deal.
zh: 感觉像是一件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I think it actually may save their margins, because right now all these companies are rushing to add a agent to their offering and thinking, "Oh, the agent is going to be the main way that people interact with me."
zh: 嗯，我认为这实际上可能会挽救他们的利润率，因为现在所有这些公司都在急于向他们的产品中添加一个智能体并认为，“噢，智能体将成为人们与我交互的主要方式。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that cost tokens, obviously.
zh: 而这显然需要消耗 token。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I actually think once I have Codex or Cowork as my main work surface, I still want to use SaaS.
zh: 而且我真的认为一旦我拥有 Codex 或 Cowork 作为我的主要工作界面，我仍然想要使用 SaaS。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, this is another good prediction.
zh: 所以，这是另一个很好的预测。

speaker: Unknown
en: I would buy SaaS stocks right now.
zh: 我现在会买入 SaaS 股票。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the SaaS apocalypse is dumb, and SaaS stocks will be up majorly in the next couple years.
zh: 我认为 SaaS 末日论是愚蠢的，SaaS 股票在未来几年将会大幅上涨。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not investment advice, but I would buy SaaS stocks.
zh: 不是投资建议，但我会买入 SaaS 股票。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, I think it saves your margin because now the way that you're thinking then is not I have to build AI into this, it's more like I have to make a piece of software that humans and AI want to collaborate on together.
zh: 所以，我认为这挽救了你的利润率，因为现在你的思维方式不是必须将 AI 构建到其中，它更像是必须制作一款人类和 AI 想要协作的软件。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's hard, but once you build it, it's a lot cheaper than assuming everyone's spending tokens, and I think it's a good business.
zh: 这很难，但一旦你构建了它，它比假设每个人都在花费 token 要便宜得多，我认为这是一个好生意。

speaker: Unknown
en: And part of the reason I'm so bullish on SaaS is A, everybody internally here is, like I said, we have all got agents and we're all using Codex and whatever, and we still pay for a ton of SaaS and our SaaS spend is up year over year, and we're not vibe coding every single little thing.
zh: 我对 SaaS 如此看好的部分原因是 A，这里内部的每个人，就像我说过的，我们都有智能体，我们都在使用 Codex 和其他的，我们仍然为大量的 SaaS 付费，而且我们的 SaaS 支出同比增长了，我们并不是在氛围编码每一件小事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that what agents do is increase the number of users of SaaS, not get rid of it.
zh: 我认为智能体所做的是增加 SaaS 的用户数量，而不是摆脱它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, I think SaaS companies are going to see an insane spike in the amount of demand that they have, because there's going to be tons of agents using these products at a very high volume.
zh: 所以，我认为 SaaS 公司将会看到他们需求数量的疯狂飙升，因为将有大量的智能体以非常高的量级使用这些产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And like I said, that's a huge infrastructure challenge.
zh: 就像我说的，这是一个巨大的基础设施挑战。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of interesting pricing challenges, but it makes me very bullish on SaaS.
zh: 有很多有趣的定价挑战，但这让我对 SaaS 非常看好。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that.
zh: 我喜欢那一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: If anything else comes out of this conversation, Dan Shipper, SaaS is the future of AI.
zh: 如果这次谈话还有其他结果的话，那就是 Dan Shipper，SaaS 是 AI 的未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: B2B SaaS.
zh: B2B SaaS。

speaker: Unknown
en: Hashtag send tweet.
zh: 话题标签发推文。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, this is quite contrarian.
zh: 是的，这相当反主流。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the other interesting piece is that the fact that you guys are hiring, that you doubled in people in the past year, which is not what people would have expected from a company that is so AI forward.
zh: 另一个有趣的部分是，你们正在招聘这一事实，你们在过去一年里人员翻了一番，这并不是人们对一家如此 AI 先进的公司所期望的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about your experience there of just, okay, we still actually need humans.
zh: 谈谈你在那方面的经验，好吧，我们实际上仍然需要人类。

speaker: Unknown
en: Automation is a lie, in the sense that every time you automate something, in order to make sure the automation is working well you need a human on top of it making sure that it's working well.
zh: 自动化是个谎言，某种意义上，每次你自动化某样东西时，为了确保自动化运行良好，你需要一个人在上面确保它运行良好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, I wrote this piece a couple years ago about the allocation economy, the idea that the way that humans are going to work with AI is going to be like being a manager.
zh: 所以，几年前我写了这篇关于分配经济的文章，这个观点是人类与 AI 共事的方式就像是一个经理。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the thing that you have to remember about managers is like managers actually spend a lot of time working.
zh: 关于经理你必须记住的一点是，经理实际上花大量时间工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most managers are not on the beach, they're checking in with their employees all the time and trying to figure out, "Okay, how do we make this work good?
zh: 大多数经理不在海滩上，他们一直在与员工联系并试图弄清楚，“好吧，我们如何让这个工作做得好？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do we make it better?
zh: 我们如何让它变得更好？

speaker: Unknown
en: How's it doing?
zh: 它做得怎么样？

speaker: Unknown
en: How's this person doing?"
zh: 这个人做得怎么样？”

speaker: Unknown
en: All that kind of stuff.
zh: 诸如此类的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think there's some differences between being a human manager and being a model manager, but fundamentally it still requires a lot of time and attention.
zh: 我认为作为人类经理和作为模型经理之间存在一些差异，但根本上它仍然需要大量的时间和关注。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that we miss that in the model discourse.
zh: 我认为我们在模型讨论中忽略了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of the reasons is, benchmarks make it look like AI is more autonomous than it is.
zh: 原因之一是，基准测试让 AI 看起来比实际上更自主。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by autonomy, I mean something specific by autonomy, and I'm going to try to express this.
zh: 关于自主性，我指的是某种特定的意思，我将试着表达这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a little hard to express, but I learned this for myself because I've been feeling this paradox a little bit.
zh: 这有点难以表达，但我自己学到了这一点，因为我一直有点感觉到这种悖论。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've been feeling that we have so much automation, so much AI, and I also work way more.
zh: 我一直感觉到我们要么有太多的自动化，太多的 AI，而且我工作得也更努力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think part of the paradox started to resolve for me a little bit when I made my own benchmark.
zh: 我认为当我制作自己的基准测试时，悖论的一部分开始有点为我解决了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I made this senior, it's called the senior engineer benchmark, and it's like how good is AI versus a human engineer?
zh: 所以我制作了这个高级的，它叫高级工程师基准测试，就是 AI 与人类工程师相比有多好？

speaker: Unknown
en: And the way that I built it is again, have this app, Proof.
zh: 我构建它的方式是，再次拥有这个应用，Proof。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just vibe coded it on the side while running the rest of Every.
zh: 我只是在运营 Every 其余部分的同时在旁边进行 vibe coded。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when we launched it, because it was completely vibe coded, it just started going down and I couldn't fix it and it was very embarrassing.
zh: 当我们发布它时，因为它完全是 vibe coded 的，它开始出问题，我无法修复它，这非常尴尬。

speaker: Unknown
en: I had a lot of egg on my face.
zh: 我颜面扫地。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the product worked, we tested it internally.
zh: 产品有效，我们在内部进行了测试。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had a lot of beta testers, but the day after launch it was like just every 10 minutes the servers would go down and people were looking at me and I'd be like, "I don't know what's going on.
zh: 我们有很多 Beta 测试人员，但发布后的第二天，就像每 10 分钟服务器就会宕机，人们看着我，我会说，“我不知道发生了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Codex, fix it."
zh: Codex，修复它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And Codex is like, "I don't know what's going on."
zh: Codex 就会说，“我不知道发生了什么。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Or really Codex was like, "I do know what's going on, I fixed it."
zh: 或者实际上 Codex 会说，“我知道发生了什么，我修复了它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then it would cause four other errors and then you're just going around in a circle, and I wasn't sleeping, and I vibe coded so hard I got bursitis on my elbow.
zh: 然后它会导致四个其他错误，然后你只是在兜圈子，我没在睡觉，我 vibe coded 得太用力，我的肘部得了滑囊炎。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, there's a life lesson in there.
zh: 所以，这里面有一个人生教训。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vibe coder elbow.
zh: Vibe coder 肘。

speaker: Unknown
en: So anyway, I got actually two different senior engineers to fix it independently.
zh: 所以无论如何，我实际上找了两个不同的高级工程师独立修复它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, I have two different rewrites of the code base that tells me how they did it.
zh: 所以，我有两个不同的代码库重写版本，告诉我他们是如何做到的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, what I get to do is when we get new models, I just give the new model a prompt.
zh: 所以，我能做的是，当我们获得新模型时，我只是给新模型一个提示。

speaker: Unknown
en: I say like, "This is vibe coded slop.
zh: 我会这样说，“这是 vibe coded 的烂摊子。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you wanted to rewrite it from first principles, how would you rewrite it?
zh: 如果你想从头开始重写它，你会如何重写它？

speaker: Unknown
en: Go do it."
zh: 去做吧。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And all the models until GPT 5.5 got like a 30 out of 100, and a human senior engineer gets like high 80s, low 90s out of 100.
zh: 直到 GPT 5.5 的所有模型都得到了 100 分里的 30 分，而人类高级工程师得到 100 分里的 80 多分、90 低分。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, there's a lot to go.
zh: 所以，还有很长的路要走。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I tried GPT 5.5 and it got like a 62.
zh: 然后我尝试了 GPT 5.5，它得到了 62 分左右。

speaker: Unknown
en: And mind you, the 60 score was GPT 5.5 using an Opus 4.7 plan.
zh: 请注意，60 分的分数是 GPT 5.5 使用 Opus 4.7 计划获得的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Opus 4.7 plans are very good, GPT 5.5 is the only model though that has the sense of agency and confidence to just like rip out old code and just actually rewrite from first principles.
zh: Opus 4.7 计划非常好，GPT 5.5 是唯一具有智能体感和信心去删除旧代码并真正从头开始重写的模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: Other coding models, they end up papering over the edges or around the edges, and they're like, "Oh, this is a big job.
zh: 其他编码模型，它们最终会在边缘或周围进行修补，它们会说，“噢，这是个大工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'll just do a little patch."
zh: 我只会做一点补丁。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you're like, "No, I specifically told you not to."
zh: 你会说，“不，我明确告诉过你不要这样做。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So GPT 5.5, there's like a 30 point bump in the score, 60 out of 100.
zh: 所以 GPT 5.5，分数有 30 分的提升，100 分里得 60 分。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very clear that in a year or less it's going to be senior engineer level, and that gives you a certain picture in your mind, especially based on how I named the benchmark, which I think a lot of benchmarks do.
zh: 很明显，在一年或更短时间内，它将达到高级工程师的水平，这给了你一个心理上的画面，特别是根据我命名基准测试的方式，我认为很多基准测试都是这样做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I can tell you that when we get to that point, it will be very easy for me to change the benchmark to zero out the current model, so that gets a zero out of 100.
zh: 我可以告诉你，当我们到达那个点时，对我来说，很容易将基准测试更改为将当前的模型归零，这样它就会得到 0 分（满分 100）。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so for example, it seems like there's no skill or no thought into the prompt, which is this is vibe coder slop.
zh: 举个例子，看起来在提示词中没有任何技巧或思考，这就是那种氛围式编码员的垃圾。

speaker: Unknown
en: Fix it from first principles.
zh: 从第一性原理开始修复它。

speaker: Unknown
en: But actually it took me a while to get to a prompt that didn't give away the answer, but got the model to reveal what it's capable of.
zh: 但实际上，我花了一段时间才找到一个不会泄露答案，但能让模型展示其能力的提示词。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the original prompt I gave it was the original prompt that I gave it when I was trying to fix the issue and production was going down, which is like I had woken up in the morning and I was like, "Okay, we had four or five reported issues yesterday.
zh: 我最初给出的提示词是我在试图修复问题且生产环境出现故障时给出的原始提示词，就像我早上醒来然后说，“好吧，我们昨天有四五个报告的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want you to go through all the issues and then make a plan for how to resolve all of them, and go do it."
zh: 我希望你把所有问题都过一遍，然后制定一个解决所有问题的计划，并去执行。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And every coding model on the market, and I'm pretty sure this ...
zh: 市场上的每个编码模型，而且我很确信这……

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's a prediction, I'm pretty sure every coding model on the market will still do this in a year.
zh: 这里有个预测，我很确信市场上的每个编码模型一年后仍然会这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every coding model in the market will take that instruction seriously.
zh: 市场上的每个编码模型都会认真对待那个指令。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if I tell it, "Here's a bunch of issues, go fix it," they will just go try to fix the issues.
zh: 如果我告诉它，“这里有一堆问题，去修复它”，它们就会去尝试修复这些问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: What an actual human senior engineer does is they go look at the code base and they're like, "This is a piece of shit.
zh: 一个真正的人类高级工程师做的是，他们会查看代码库然后说，“这简直是坨屎。

speaker: Unknown
en: This guy doesn't know what he's doing."
zh: 这家伙不知道自己在做什么。”

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: I was just talking to a data science friend and he was saying how his team is just, his data science team is just, their job used to be do analysis, answer questions, see if this experiment was a good, was positive.
zh: 我刚才在和一个数据科学朋友聊天，他说他的团队，他的数据科学团队只是，他们的工作曾经是做分析、回答问题、看看这个实验是不是好的、是不是积极的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now it's just everyone's doing that and they're sharing their results and they're like, "No, this is not correct."
zh: 现在每个人都在做那个，他们在分享他们的结果，他们就像，“不，这是不正确的。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And most of their job is now reviewing bad data science work.
zh: 他们现在的大部分工作都在审查糟糕的数据科学工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Which is a problem and it means that, and the same thing is happening with engineers and it means that you need more, like you actually need that engineers for this and you need data scientists and it means that you haven't set up the appropriate systems or agents to help you with this.
zh: 这是一个问题，这意味着，同样的事情正在工程师身上发生，这意味着你需要更多，比如你实际上需要为此工程师，你需要数据科学家，这意味着你没有建立适当的系统或智能体来帮助你处理这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the way that it works inside of the big model companies, for example, at least one of them has literally a data science bot that every single person in the org can query that is hooked up to their data warehouse that knows who's who so that it knows at the warehouse level who has permission to access what.
zh: 所以在大型模型公司内部的工作方式，例如，至少有一家公司确实有一个数据科学机器人，组织里的每个人都可以查询它，它连接到他们的数据仓库，知道谁是谁，以便它知道在仓库级别谁有权限访问什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so all of the basic questions, because there's a team that sets up this bot, all of the basic questions that people might want to ask that it sometimes that might get wrong, that they're constantly making sure it's getting it right.
zh: 所以所有基本的问题，因为有一个团队建立了这个机器人，人们可能想问的所有基本问题，它有时可能会弄错，他们不断确保它做对。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the data science team doesn't have to answer all the bullshit questions because there's another team building an agent that is set up to do that really well.
zh: 所以数据科学团队不必回答所有废话问题，因为有另一个团队在建立一个智能体，该智能体被设置为很好地做到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: But if the team didn't exist, the data scientists would hate their lives.
zh: 但如果这个团队不存在，数据科学家会讨厌他们的生活。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It does though make the job maybe less fun because you're just sitting there gardening people's sloppy work versus-

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, that's what I think is like it can actually make the job better because for the data scientists, you are now not dealing with all the silly requests.
zh: 嗯，我认为这实际上可以让工作变得更好，因为对于数据科学家来说，你现在不用处理所有愚蠢的请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're dealing with the deeper questions that are harder for the team who's dealing with all the basic requests and building an agent to do that.
zh: 你正在处理那些更深层次的问题，这些问题对于处理所有基本请求和构建智能体来做这件事的团队来说更难。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like filtering all that stuff out so you can focus.
zh: 就像是把所有这些东西过滤掉，这样你就可以专注。

en: Here's a question I've been thinking about.
zh: 我一直在思考的一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was not planning to talk about this, but it's something that I've been thinking about.

zh: 那么问题在于，哪个产品技术角色目前变化最小？

speaker: Unknown

zh: 产品管理，很多 PRD，你不需要写那么多，你可以交付代码，你不需要等待别人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Design, the whole design process dead according to recent guests, just like there's no time to do the whole design process, very different role.
zh: 设计，整个设计流程根据最近的嘉宾来说已经死了，就像根本没有时间做整个设计流程，非常不同的角色。

speaker: Unknown
en: Data science, very different work now.
zh: 数据科学，现在是非常不同的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's marketing, there's sales.
zh: 还有市场营销，还有销售。

speaker: Unknown
en: So here's the question, what do you think is the least fundamentally changed role so far?
zh: 所以问题是，你们认为到目前为止哪个角色在根本上的变化最小？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, on interesting thing is, I don't know if this counts, but CEOs and investors, it seems still very, very optional whether or not they use this stuff.
zh: 嗯，有一件有趣的事是，我不知道这算不算，但 CEO 和投资者，他们是否使用这些东西似乎还是非常非常可选的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It seems that way.
zh: 看起来是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the opposite is actually true.

en: My experience, and we do a lot of this with senior executives and senior leadership teams, my experience is that your company's only going to go as far as your CEO goes in AI and it's not something you can delegate.
zh: 根据我的经验，我们确实与高管和高级领导团队做了很多这方面的合作，我的经验是，你的公司在 AI 方面只能走得跟你的 CEO 一样远，而且这不是你可以委派的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to have your hands in it because otherwise you don't have an intuition for it.

speaker: Unknown
en: But for a long time it has seemed like, yeah, that's something that the people who are doing the work have to do, but I don't have to do that.
zh: 但在很长一段时间里，看起来好像，是的，那是做工作的人必须做的事情，但我不需要做那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'll just tell them what to do.

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think if you're a CEO, you kind of can get away with your day looking very similar.
zh: 所以我认为如果你是一名 CEO，你基本上可以度过看起来非常相似的一天。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that will change rapidly at some point where it'll be like, "Oh no, I'm way behind."

speaker: Unknown
en: But for now, or maybe even middle managers, those kinds of people, I think it's fairly similar.

en: I think maybe sales because it's so in person.

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, that's my vote.
zh: 是的，那是我的投票。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's sort of creeping up in the kind of BDR.

speaker: Unknown
en: We can deal with a lot of BDR type queries.
zh: 我们可以处理很多 BDR 类型的查询。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're only talking to people who actually want it.
zh: 你只跟那些真正想要它的人交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: For sales, it's so useful to do research.
zh: 对于销售来说，做研究非常有用。

speaker: Unknown
en: My favorite Codex, one of my favorite Codex experiences is we're hiring a head of L&D and we always put out a job post, whatever, but I was like, "I feel like there's this company called General Assembly in New York and they've done really good technology education for a long time."
zh: 我最喜欢的一个 Codex 经历，也是我最喜欢的 Codex 经历之一，当时我们正在招聘一名 L&D 负责人，我们总是发布一个职位帖子，不管怎样，但是我想，“我觉得纽约有一家公司叫 General Assembly，他们做技术教育已经做得很好很长时间了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I was like, "I feel like someone who worked at General Assembly and is now into AI would be really good."
zh: 所以我想，“我觉得一个在 General Assembly 工作过并且现在从事 AI 的人会非常棒。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I just literally typed it into Codex and then went off and was doing something else and I came back and it found this the perfect guy.
zh: 我真的只是把它打进了 Codex，然后去忙别的了，当我回来的时候，它找到了这个完美的家伙。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was like worked at General Assembly, was an instructor, is super AI-pilled and follows me on Twitter.
zh: 他曾在 General Assembly 工作，是一名讲师，超级 AI-pilled（沉迷 AI），并且在 Twitter 上关注我。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I just DM'd him and then I had dinner with him and it's like, that's crazy.

speaker: Unknown
en: That would've taken so long before and super valuable for sales, for recruiting, all that kind of stuff.
zh: 那以前要花很长时间，对于销售、招聘，所有这类事情都非常有价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.

speaker: Unknown
en: Sales is where my mind went.

speaker: Unknown
en: The top of funnel AI is helping a lot with sourcing and qualifying things like that.
zh: 漏斗顶部 AI 在寻找线索和资格认定这类事情上帮助很大。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like the work of a salesperson is not fundamentally different and
zh: 感觉销售人员的工作并没有根本上的不同，而且

speaker: Unknown
en: Customer support's fundamentally changed.
zh: 客户支持已经根本性地改变了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's interesting.

speaker: Unknown
en: Sales so far so good for those folks.
zh: 销售到目前为止对那些人来说还算好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So maybe just summarizing some of the predictions in this bucket of just the shape of the work, how it's going to change.
zh: 所以也许只是总结一下这一类中的一些关于工作形状、它将如何变化的预测。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I'm hearing so far is just going to be a lot more reviewing of other people's output as a part of the work.
zh: 我目前听到的是，作为工作的一部分，将会有更多对其他人产出的审查。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then two, there's going to be a lot of like almost babysitting of AI agents to make them do the thing you want them to do for deploying and then just gardening them along the way, make sure they continue to do their work.
zh: 然后第二，会有很多几乎是 babysitting（照看） AI 智能体，为了让它们做你想要它们做的事情用于部署，然后只是 gardening（维护）它们，确保它们继续做它们的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Anything else before we get into our third bucket?
zh: 在我们进入第三个类别之前还有什么别的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I would sort of split it into less babysitting agents and more your forward deployed team is trying to build a whole system that makes it so that people who have less knowledge can use that system without doing something dumb.
zh: 我会把它区分为更少的照看智能体，更多的你的 forward deployed（前置部署）团队正在试图构建一个完整的系统，以便让那些知识较少的人可以使用该系统而不会做蠢事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's like a really interesting engineering challenge.
zh: 这是一个非常有趣的工程挑战。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think babysitting kind of makes it feel like it's, yeah, you're just kind of waiting for it to fuck up and then fixing it or whatever.
zh: 我认为照看让它感觉像是，是的，你只是等着它搞砸然后修复它或者其他的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that can be the case, but I think a lot of it is this extremely interesting engineering challenge of building a system to enable everybody else in your organization to do what used to be a technical job.
zh: 情况可能就是这样，但我认为其中很多是一个非常有趣的工程挑战，即构建一个系统来让你组织中的其他人去做以前是技术工作的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then if you're not one of those people, like you're the data scientist or whatever, you can go a lot deeper with AI into really important questions that eventually probably filter into the work that the forward deployed engineering team is doing, but is like more generative and more new and you're dealing with harder questions.
zh: 如果你不是那些人之一，比如你是数据科学家或者其他的，你可以在 AI 方面深入得多，进入真正重要的问题，这些问题最终可能会过滤到前置部署工程团队的工作中，但更具生成性、更新颖，你正在处理更难的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: One last thing that I think is really interesting is I think that we will be reading way more AI generated writing in documents and emails and we will like it.
zh: 我认为还有最后一件事情非常有意思，我认为我们将在文档和电子邮件中阅读更多由 AI 生成的写作，并且我们会喜欢它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think we are already doing this in coding where we read plan documents.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: That would be very silly.
zh: 那将会是非常愚蠢的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It would be obviously silly.
zh: 那显然会很愚蠢。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the same is true when we did our quarterly planning for every at the end of 2025, we did it all with Notion Agents and we just had a bunch of Notion agents and we had really one Notion agent and then we had a top level company strategy and then we had everybody in the company just talked to an agent and it asked them about what happened last year, how did it go, what were your goals?
zh: 而且我认为同样的情况也是真的，当我们在 2025 年底进行每季度规划时，我们都是用 Notion 智能体完成的，我们只是有一堆 Notion 智能体，我们要说真的只有一个 Notion 智能体，然后我们要有一个顶层的公司战略，然后公司里的每个人都只跟一个智能体交谈，它询问他们去年发生了什么，进展如何，你的目标是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you want to do this year?
zh: 你今年想做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are your metrics?
zh: 你的指标是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: It pushed back and then it was like, how does this relate to the overall company idea?
zh: 它会提出反驳，然后它会问，这与公司整体的想法有什么关系？

speaker: Unknown
en: All that kind of stuff.
zh: 诸如此类的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I got these incredibly good AI generated strategy reports or quarterly plans for each part of each team.

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I could go in and be like, "Okay, who needs to talk to each other?
zh: 然后我可以进去说，“好吧，谁需要跟谁交谈？

speaker: Unknown
en: Which teams need to talk to each other that don't know they need to talk to each other?

speaker: Unknown
en: And which one of these is actually low quality or which one of these is high quality?"
zh: 而且这其中哪一个是低质量的，或者哪一个是高质量的？”

speaker: Unknown
en: All that kind of stuff, it makes it a lot easier to process.
zh: 所有这些事情，都让处理变得容易得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I see that all the time now.
zh: 而且我现在一直看到这种情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: I consistently get AI generated stuff and there's a difference between an AI generated document that's slop and not.
zh: 我 consistently 收到 AI 生成的东西，而且 AI 生成的文档是垃圾和不是垃圾是有区别的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the slap one is it took them less time to make it than it takes me to read it and they don't stand behind every line.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

zh: 如果我们谈论它，并且很明显你不知道里面有什么，那是个大大的禁忌，不允许那样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think we have this aversion to AI-generated stuff that will go away because the kind of strategy document that GBT 5.5 can write when it's directed well by someone on my team is way better than them just like dinking and dunking their fingers on the keyboard.
zh: 我认为我们对 AI 生成的东西有一种厌恶，这种厌恶会消失，因为当 GBT 5.5 被我团队中的某个人很好地指导时，它能写出的那种战略文档比他们只是在键盘上 dinking and dunking 手指要好得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: Right.
zh: 对。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most people are really bad at writing strategy documents.

speaker: Unknown
en: The bar is low.
zh: 门槛很低。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And same thing with email.

speaker: Unknown
en: Most of my email is written by GPT 5.5 in Codex right now.

speaker: Unknown
en: And I honestly would prefer it to say that it's coming from GPT 5.5 and I may change it to do that.
zh: 而老实说，我更愿意它说是来自 GPT 5.5，我可能会改成像那样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I had this experience the other day where I had to send an email to one of our investors and I asked Codex to go do it.
zh: 但前几天我有这个经历，我必须给我们的一个投资者发一封邮件，我让 Codex 去做。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Codex knows to ask me and it usually does, but this time it didn't and it just sent the email and I didn't look at it at all and I was like, "Fuck."

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I went to my sent and looked at it and I was like, "Oh, this is exactly what I would've sent."

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it's pretty close to that a lot of the time.
zh: 所以很多时候它都非常接近那个水平。

speaker: Unknown
en: It can be a little overformal and there's a couple of things that...

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just when you really think about it, most of your email is kind of...

speaker: Unknown
en: It's kind of wrote.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

zh: 我真的认为，从结构上讲，由于模型的工作方式，由于模型公司为了让它们合规和对齐而存在的经济激励，结构上它们总是会被那些利用模型来制造新专业知识或制造以前从未以这种方式做过的新事物的人甩在后面，以适应他们非常、非常特定的情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that stuff is going to get incorporated into the models, but again, it will create room for people to push further ahead.

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that you see this in a small way in pretty much all the jobs is engineers.

speaker: Unknown
en: We've talked about how Codex and Cowork are becoming the sort of standard operating system for work.
zh: 我们已经谈论过 Codex 和 Cowork 如何正在成为工作的标准操作系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're just doing that and when new models come out, you're trying them and figuring out, okay, now there are new powers.

speaker: Unknown
en: How can I use them?

speaker: Unknown
en: Instead of just being like, "I'm going to try to ignore it because it makes me afraid."

en: Which I think is honestly, it's rational.

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, one of the things that's really interesting is a lot of companies handicap their employees from even doing this because I don't know if you can use the latest models at Salesforce.

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of times you have to wait or it's whatever.

speaker: Unknown
en: So maybe you have to do it in your off time.
zh: 所以也许你必须利用业余时间来做。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the thing that I really like to do with new models is play, and there are certain things where I know it can't quite do it yet, but when a new model comes out, I always turn the rock over again to be like, "Can I do it now?"

speaker: Unknown
en: So it could not do the senior engineer benchmark last time, and I turned the rock over again and now it's at a 60 out of 100, which is really good.

speaker: Unknown
en: So the way to ride the models is not one specific thing because they're always changing, but it is to be curious and playful to apply the model, the new model to whatever it is that you care about, whether that's your job or something outside of your job and to keep turning over rocks because it may not work now, but it may work eventually.
zh: 所以驾驭模型的方式不是一件具体的事情，因为它们总是在变化，而是要保持好奇心和玩耍心去应用模型，即新模型，应用到你关心的任何事情上，无论是你的工作还是工作之外的事情，并不断翻开石头，因为它现在可能不起作用，但最终可能会起作用。

speaker: Unknown
en: It probably will work eventually and the way that you use it matters.
zh: 它最终很可能会起作用，而且你使用它的方式很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what's really cool is that I think people think of the edge of AI as being in San Francisco, and I actually don't think that that's where it is.
zh: 所以真的很酷的一点是，我认为人们认为 AI 的前沿是在旧金山，但实际上我不认为是在那里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the edge of AI is wherever AI meets a real human doing something because the people at San Francisco, they're making it, but they don't actually know a lot about how to use it.
zh: 我认为 AI 的前沿是 AI 遇到真正做某事的人的地方，因为在旧金山的人，他们在制造它，但他们实际上并不太了解如何使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't know, or at least they don't know everything about how to use it.
zh: 他们不知道，或者至少他们不完全了解如何使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: They need to see how other people use it.
zh: 他们需要看到其他人如何使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so whenever a new model comes out, you get to be one of the first people in the world to discover what it might be useful for.
zh: 因此，每当有新模型发布时，你都会成为世界上第一批发现它可能有什么用途的人之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like a new discovery.
zh: 这就像一个新发现。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's why, for example, we're in Brooklyn, but I really think of us, and I think we are quite far ahead of people in San Francisco because we just use them for everything.
zh: 我认为这就是为什么，例如，我们在布鲁克林，但我真的认为我们，我认为我们比旧金山的人领先很多，因为我们把 AI 用于一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if people do that consistently, I think it's going to be very hard to lose.
zh: 如果人们坚持这样做，我认为很难会输。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is one of the most amazing things about AI right now is no matter how much money you have or a little money you have, you have access to the most advanced AI model.
zh: 这就是目前关于 AI 最令人惊奇的事情之一，无论你有多少钱或很少钱，你都能获得最先进的 AI 模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not free, so you need some money and you can get it immediately when it comes out.
zh: 它不是免费的，所以你需要一些钱，你可以在它发布时立即得到它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe the only people that have an advantage are the people working at OpenAI or Anthropic, but otherwise it's just available.
zh: 也许唯一有优势的人是在 OpenAI 或 Anthropic 工作的人，否则它就是可用的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know.
zh: 我知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was at their event with you, their Code with Claude event with you last week or a couple weeks ago and they're all using mythos and I'm like, "God, damn it."
zh: 我上周还是几周前和你一起参加了他们的活动，和你一起参加了他们的 Code with Claude 活动，他们都在使用 mythos，我就想，“该死。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so annoying, but I think that's totally true.
zh: 真的很烦人，但我认为那完全是正确的。

speaker: Unknown
en: If IBM had invented AI, you can bet it would not be like this, and it would be a bajillion dollars and only the top companies could use it and they would be using it in the weirdest, most uninteresting ways.
zh: 如果 IBM 发明了 AI，你可以肯定它不会像这样，它要花费几十亿美元，只有顶尖的公司才能使用它，而且他们会以最奇怪、最无趣的方式使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really important that AI was built in America and in the Silicon Valley culture that's like, "We want to make intelligence too cheap to meter."
zh: AI 在美国并在硅谷文化中建立非常重要，这种文化是，“我们要让智能廉价到无法计量。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That's not the default stance.
zh: 这不是默认的立场。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it means that everyone has this broadly accessible tool that they can use and I think that's amazing.
zh: 这意味着每个人都有这个广泛可用的工具可以使用，我认为这很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's such a good point.
zh: 这是一个非常好的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And interestingly, it's also created the most fastest growing companies in history, the biggest companies in history.
zh: 有趣的是，它也创造了历史上增长最快的公司，历史上最大的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's true.
zh: 那是真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Those Silicon Valley guys, they're smart.
zh: 那些硅谷的人，他们很聪明。

speaker: Unknown
en: If I zoom out on the conversation, it's really interesting.
zh: 如果我缩小视角来看这次对话，真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's kind of these two sides to the coin.
zh: 这就像硬币的两面。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is not a lot is actually, so much is not changing.
zh: 一面是实际上没怎么变，很多东西没有改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: SaaS continues, jobs not disappearing.
zh: SaaS 在继续，工作没有消失。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're still emailing each other.
zh: 我们还在互相发邮件。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're still working on Slack.
zh: 我们还在 Slack 上工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of the work not changed.
zh: 很多工作没有改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the other hand, every role transformed, engineers don't write code, PMs don't write PRDs, design and design.
zh: 另一方面，每个角色都转变了，工程师不写代码，产品经理不写 PRD，设计和设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so interesting how much has changed, how much has not changed.
zh: 有趣的是多少改变了，多少没有改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know.
zh: 我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's interesting that people think it's going to be this whole new world, but in many ways it's, okay, it'll continue the way it is with a lot of stuff around the edges.
zh: 有趣的是人们认为这将是一个全新的世界，但在很多方面它是，好吧，它将继续保持现在的方式，只是在边缘有很多东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's how I feel.
zh: 这就是我的感觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm simultaneously so excited and it feels like everything has changed, and I'm so bullish on it and the progress that we're making, all that kind of stuff.
zh: 我同时感到非常兴奋，感觉一切都变了，我对它非常看好，对我们正在取得的进步，所有这类事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And, yeah, I feel like there are these things where they're going to be pretty similar to how they are and that's probably good.
zh: 而且，是的，我觉得有些事情它们将与现在非常相似，这可能是好事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think generally our intuitions about the future, the model that I have of what our intuitions are about the future is the intuitions that people had in the Middle Ages about what happened at the end of the horizon.
zh: 我认为通常我们对未来的直觉，我对我们的直觉是什么的模型是人们在中世纪对地平线尽头发生的事情的直觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, are there dragons?
zh: 就像，有龙吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Does it drop off into nothingness or whatever?
zh: 它是会逐渐消逝直到虚无还是怎样？

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of people have a lot of deep intuition that there's something terrible going to happen over the horizon and also that some people are like, "There's something incredible.
zh: 很多人都有一种强烈的直觉，认为地平线上会发生什么可怕的事情，而且也有一些人会说，「有什么不可思议的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's going to change everything.
zh: 它将改变一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're all going to be happy.
zh: 我们都会很开心。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a utopia."
zh: 这是一个乌托邦。」

speaker: Unknown
en: And what happens is you get there and you're like, there's some really cool things, there's some not cool things and it's just another horizon.
zh: 而当你到了那里，你会发现有一些真的很酷的事情，也有一些不酷的事情，而这只是另一个地平线。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's the way to think about the future.
zh: 我认为这就是思考未来的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And until you get to that place where you're starting to see it, and I think we get to see it because we get to see it internally all the time.
zh: 直到你到达那个地方，开始看到它，我认为我们能见到它，因为我们一直在内部见到它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's important not to let your mind get away from you and being like, "This is going to happen and this is going to happen and whatever."
zh: 重要的是不要让你的思绪失控，不要去想，「这将会发生，那将会发生，或者其他的什么。」

speaker: Unknown
en: Because you're going to tell a story that sounds so real in the moment, but later on you're like, "Actually it's much more complex than that."
zh: 因为你会讲一个在当下听起来非常真实的故事，但后来你会想，「实际上这比那要复杂得多。」

speaker: Unknown
en: And somewhere it's sort of a both, everything's changed and nothing has.
zh: 在某种程度上，这是一种两者并存的状态，一切都变了，但什么也没变。

speaker: Unknown
en: And once you get there, I think you're sort of starting to see like, "Oh, yeah, this is a real thing."
zh: 一旦你到了那里，我想你开始会看到，「噢，是的，这是真实的东西。」

speaker: Unknown
en: Part of it is the AI companies are very good at scaring us about what might happen in the future.
zh: 部分原因是 AI 公司非常擅长用未来可能发生的事情来吓唬我们。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's actually shifting.
zh: 我认为这实际上正在转变。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think they've realized maybe we should not freak everybody out about the dangers.
zh: 我认为他们意识到也许我们不应该让每个人都对危险感到恐慌。

speaker: Unknown
en: That PR strategy just does not make any sense to me.
zh: 这种公关策略对我来说完全讲不通。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do think that it's genuine, but it's so ineffective, and I think it's also wrong.
zh: 我确实认为它是真诚的，但它太无效了，而且我认为这也是错误的。

speaker: Unknown
en: How about we end with maybe just a few things listeners should do to be successful over the next year with the way the world is moving.
zh: 我们这样结束怎么样，也许只有几件事是听众在接下来的一年里为了随着世界的运转而取得成功应该做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Ride the models.
zh: 驾驭这些模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: I would try all of your workflows in Codex or Cowork and see how that works.
zh: 我会在 Codex 或 Cowork 中尝试你所有的流程，看看效果如何。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if your company doesn't let you do it on your own time, I would try out some of these agent products like OpenClaw or Hermes or for less technical people, there's like Victor, we have OnePlus ones.
zh: 如果你的公司不允许你在自己的时间里做这件事，我会尝试一些像 OpenClaw 或 Hermes 这样的智能体产品，或者对于技术性较弱的人，有像 Victor 这样的，我们有 OnePlus ones。

speaker: Unknown
en: I would get comfortable with both of those ways of working and try to have fun.
zh: 我会习惯这两种工作方式，并试着找点乐子。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think there's too much of I'm doing this because I have FOMO, I might lose my job or I might miss out on this big thing or whatever.
zh: 我认为太多的情况是，我做这件事是因为我有错失恐惧症，我可能会失去我的工作，或者我可能会错过这件大事，或者其他的什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the best way to actually figure out interesting, useful things to do with AI is to do something enjoyable.
zh: 而实际上找出用 AI 做的有用、有趣的事情的最好方法，就是做一件令人愉快的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Nikhil Singal was on the podcast and the way he described it is you got to find your moment of joy with AI once you find like, "Wow, I can't believe AI did this for me.
zh: Nikhil Singal 来过播客，他描述的方式是你必须在 AI 中找到你的快乐时刻，一旦你发现，「哇，我简直不敢相信 AI 为我做了这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is awesome.
zh: 这太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to keep building stuff."
zh: 我们要继续构建东西。」

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I agree.
zh: 是的，我同意。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you haven't seen that yet, then it's just like, try, fine, try solving it.
zh: 所以如果你还没看到那个，那就试着，好吧，试着解决它。

speaker: Unknown
en: The thing I hear a lot is just find a problem in your life or work and see if AI can do it.
zh: 我听到很多的一句话就是，在你生活中或工作中找一个问题，看看 AI 能不能做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Go to Lovable, go to apply code, go to Replit, just try to build the thing and often it's like, holy shit, this is so cool."
zh: 去 Lovable，去 apply code，去 Replit，试着构建那个东西，通常就像，天哪，这太酷了。」

speaker: Unknown
en: Dan, is there anything else that we haven't covered?
zh: Dan，还有什么我们没有覆盖到的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: We've gone deep on so much.
zh: 我们深入探讨了很多事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there anything else you wanted to share?
zh: 还有什么你想分享的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Anything else you want to predict or just say before we get to a very exciting lightning round?
zh: 在我们进入非常激动人心的闪电轮之前，还有什么你想预测的或者想说的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think we covered it.
zh: 我想我们都覆盖到了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We did a lot.
zh: 我们做了很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is awesome, and I'm very excited to see how well or poorly I do in a year, and I hope that you hold me to it.
zh: 这太棒了，我很兴奋想看看一年后我做得好还是不好，我希望你能监督我。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to have AI score us.
zh: 我们要让 AI 给我们打分。

speaker: Unknown
en: How about that?
zh: 怎么样？

speaker: Unknown
en: Great.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Look at the world like a dance prediction.
zh: 像看待舞蹈预测一样看待这个世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: See where it goes.
zh: 看看它会去向何方。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, with that, Dan Shipper, we've reached a very exciting lightning round.
zh: 好了，就到这里，Dan Shipper，我们到了一个非常激动人心的闪电轮。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've got five questions for you.
zh: 我有五个问题要问你。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you ready?
zh: 你准备好了吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm ready.
zh: 我准备好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are two or three books that you find yourself recommending most to other people?
zh: 有哪两三本书是你发现自己最经常推荐给别人的？

speaker: Unknown
en: Obviously Annie Dillard.
zh: 显然是 Annie Dillard。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everyone at every has to read The Writing Life.
zh: 每个人在任何时候都必须读《The Writing Life》。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you join, you get a copy and you have to read it.
zh: 当你加入时，你会得到一本副本，你必须读它。

speaker: Unknown
en: You only have to read the last chapter though.
zh: 不过你只需要读最后一章。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the last chapter is incredible, and it is at the intersection of writing and technology and the future and its relationship to the future and to time.
zh: 我认为最后一章非常棒，它处于写作和科技以及未来及其与未来和时间关系的交叉点上。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's everything about every wrapped up into a very tight chapter.
zh: 它把关于一切的一切都打包进了一个非常紧凑的章节。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so good.
zh: 它太好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think Annie Dillard just generally is fantastic.
zh: 我认为 Annie Dillard 总体来说就是很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: What else do I recommend?
zh: 我还推荐别的什么吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I'll just tell you a couple things that I've read that I've really liked recently, and whenever I like something, I always just tell everyone about it.
zh: 我就告诉你们几件我最近读过并且真的很喜欢的事情，每当我喜欢什么东西，我总是告诉所有人。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I have recommended these a lot.
zh: 所以我已经推荐过这些很多次了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've been reading... One of the things I learned, which I didn't know is Churchill is a really good writer and he has a whole history of World War II that he wrote and it's like a combination history and memoir.
zh: 我一直在读……我学到的其中一件事，是我之前不知道的是 Churchill 是一个非常优秀的作家，他写过一部二战史，这就像是历史和回忆录的结合体。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's so cool because he was there, he did it.
zh: 我认为这很酷，因为他在场，他做到了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's something about what we do at everywhere.
zh: 这有点像我们在 everywhere 所做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel some sort of kinship with that of like we're building stuff, we're writing stuff and it's very rare to find people that also do that.
zh: 我感觉与那种状态有某种亲属关系，就像我们在构建东西，我们在写东西，而且很难找到也这样做的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so Churchill History of World War II is fantastic.
zh: 所以 Churchill 的二战史非常棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just finished the first volume.
zh: 我刚读完第一卷。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm on the second volume.
zh: 我在读第二卷。

speaker: Unknown
en: The Nazis just invaded France.
zh: 纳粹刚刚入侵了法国。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very captivating stuff.
zh: 这是非常引人入胜的内容。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's one.
zh: 所以这是其中之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've been on a little bit of a quantum physics kick recently.
zh: 我最近有点迷恋量子物理。

speaker: Unknown
en: AI is actually very good for quantum physics if you get into it.
zh: 如果你深入进去，AI 实际上对量子物理非常有帮助。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's this book called The Rigor of Angels that I just finished, which is, it's a history of ideas that relates Heisenberg, who has his uncertainty principle.
zh: 我刚读完一本叫 The Rigor of Angels 的书，这是一部观念史，关联了海森堡，他有不不确定性原理。

speaker: Unknown
en: Borges, who's an Argentinian fiction writer, wrote a bunch of great short stories.
zh: Borges，一位阿根廷小说作家，写了很多很棒的短篇故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're actually starting to get a lot of play now because they're very AI related.
zh: 它们实际上现在开始获得很多关注，因为它们与 AI 非常相关。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Cont and very cool, super mind-blowing.
zh: 还有 Cont 和非常酷，超级震撼。

speaker: Unknown
en: Lots of interesting overlaps with AI stuff and, yeah, highly recommend.
zh: 与 AI 的东西有很多有趣的重叠，是的，强烈推荐。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like we got a whole podcast episode about your reading and books you recommend.
zh: 我感觉我们可以专门做一期播客节目来讲讲你的阅读和你推荐的书籍。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know this is a passion of yours.
zh: 我知道这是你的热情所在。

speaker: Unknown
en: My current obsession is The Power Broker.
zh: 我目前的痴迷是 The Power Broker。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think we talked about it when I was visiting you.
zh: 我想我拜访你的时候我们聊过这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just-
zh: 它只是——

speaker: Unknown
en: So good.
zh: 太好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Never ends, but it's surprisingly compelling to read through the history of New York.
zh: 永远读不完，但阅读纽约的历史出奇地引人入胜。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Second question, what is a recent movie or TV show you recently enjoyed if you have time for TV?
zh: 第二个问题，你最近喜欢的一部电影或电视节目是什么，如果你有时间看电视的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I've been watching a lot of basketball, so that's one.
zh: 所以我看了很多篮球，这是其一。

speaker: Unknown
en: I became a Knicks fan this year, so that's really fun.
zh: 我今年成了尼克斯队的粉丝，所以这很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I recently watched this, I guess it's a miniseries documentary called The Dark Wizard about this guy, Dean Potter, who he was like Alex Honnold before Alex Honnold was Alex Honnold.
zh: 但我最近看了这个，我想它是一部迷你纪录片，叫 The Dark Wizard，关于一个人叫 Dean Potter，他就是 Alex Honnold 成为 Alex Honnold 之前的 Alex Honnold。

speaker: Unknown
en: And he just has this very extreme personality where he's free soloing everything and then he's base jumping in a wingsuit and stuff like that and it's sort of exploring his psychology and what happened to him.
zh: 他有着非常极端的性格，他徒手独攀一切，然后他穿翼装做定点跳伞之类的，这有点探索他的心理和发生了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't know, I kind of like stuff like that.
zh: 我不知道，我有点喜欢那样的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's another one called Hundred Foot Wave where it's about people who are trying to select big wave surfers.
zh: 还有一个叫 Hundred Foot Wave，是关于那些试图挑选巨浪冲浪者的人。

speaker: Unknown
